title: Wetting of quantum fluids: a route to free-standing shell-shaped quantum droplets
abstract: We investigate wetting phenomena between self-bound quantum fluids in a three-component Bose mixture of $^{23}$Na, $^{39}$K, and $^{41}$K atoms. Within a density-functional approach including mean-field interactions and Lee-Huang-Yang quantum-fluctuation corrections, we consider two binary quantum liquids, formed by components $(1,2)$ and $(2,3)$, and study the adsorption of the softer $(1,2)$ liquid on a stiffer $(2,3)$ substrate. By tuning the interspecies scattering length $a_{12}$, we show that the surface tension of the $(1,2)$ liquid can be strongly varied, driving a transition from partial wetting to complete wetting of the $(2,3)$ phase. The contact angle extracted from cylindrical-cap geometries decreases continuously with increasing $a_{12}$ and vanishes near a critical value $a_{12}^{c}= -42\,a_0$. In the complete-wetting regime, a finite amount of $(1,2)$ liquid wraps around a spherical $(2,3)$ droplet, producing a self-bound core-shell droplet without external confinement, whose component-1 density has a shell-like, hollow projection. We further show that such shell-shaped quantum droplets can sustain quantized vortical excitations. These results identify wetting as a route to engineering free-standing shell-shaped quantum liquids and suggest new possibilities for studying capillarity, topology, and superfluidity in multicomponent quantum droplets.
summary: This paper demonstrates that tuning interspecies interactions in a three-component Bose mixture can drive a wetting transition, leading to the formation of self-bound, shell-shaped quantum droplets. These structures consist of a core-shell geometry where the outer component can host quantized vortices. This approach offers a new way to engineer and study the topology and capillarity of quantum fluids.
main_problem: Investigating how wetting phenomena between self-bound quantum fluids can be used to engineer free-standing, shell-shaped quantum droplets.
main_result: The study identifies a transition from partial to complete wetting in a three-component Bose mixture, where the (1,2) liquid wraps around a (2,3) core to form a self-bound core-shell droplet that can sustain quantized vortices.
method: A density-functional approach using the Mean-Field + Lee-Huang-Yang (MF+LHY) framework, solved via 3D imaginary-time evolution on a Cartesian grid.
model_or_system: A three-component Bose mixture of 23Na, 39K, and 41K atoms, modeled as two interacting binary quantum liquids where one acts as a substrate for the other.
key_observables: Surface tension, contact angle, density profiles (column density), and critical rotation frequency for quantized vortices.
important_parameters: The interspecies scattering length a12, which drives the wetting transition, and the critical value a12c = -42 a0.
main_assumptions: Zero temperature, absence of three-body recombination, and the validity of the MF+LHY approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a dimer structure in the non-wetting regime; Figure 2 shows density profiles; Figure 6 shows interfacial energy vs a12; Figure 7 shows 2D column density projections illustrating the shell structure.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of droplet engineering, describes the MF+LHY theoretical framework, details the numerical implementation, analyzes the wetting transition and contact angle scaling, demonstrates the formation of core-shell structures, and explores vortex excitations in these geometries.
why_interesting: It provides a novel mechanism (wetting) to create complex, self-bound topological structures (shell-shaped droplets) in ultracold gases, offering a platform to study superfluidity in curved, multiply connected geometries.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Total density map corresponding to the dimer struc- ture. Here a12 = −50 a0. The density values are in units of 104µm−3. The droplet (2,3) is on the left, the droplet (1,2) on the right.

FIG. 2. Density profiles of the (1,2) slab for three different values of a12 (for clarity, only one half is shown in the Figure. The solid (dashed) line shows the density of species 2 (1), respectively.

FIG. 3. Density map in a plane perpendicular to the surface plane, showing the adsorbed (1,2) cylindrical droplet on top of a slab (delimited by planar surfaces) made of the (2,3) fluid. Here a12 = −44 a0.

FIG. 4. Density maps of ρ1 + ρ2 for the equilibrium con- figurations of cylindrical cap made of fluid (1,2) on top of a planar slab of fluid (2,3), for different values of the scat- tering length a12. From left to right, from top to bottom: a12/a0 = −50, −45, −43, −42.

FIG. 5. Calculated values of the contact angles (in degrees) as a function of a12. The solid line shows the numerical fit described in the text. The vertical arrow shows the threshold value below which self-bound states of the mixture (1,2) de- velop.
